redFlags: Sender domain is hdfcbank-secure.com — not hdfcbank.com
Urgent language: suspended within 24 hours
Link goes to hdfc-verify.ru — .ru TLD unrelated to HDFC
Generic greeting: Dear Customer instead of your name
Threatens account suspension to force immediate action
explanation: This is a phishing email. HDFC Bank's real domain is hdfcbank.com — any variation like hdfcbank-secure.com is fake. The .ru destination link is a dead giveaway. Real banks never threaten suspension via email.
educationTip: Always hover over links before clicking. If the destination domain doesn't exactly match the company's official website, don't click.

data.subject: URGENT: Your HDFC Bank account will be suspended
data.body:
Dear Customer,

We have detected unusual login activity on your HDFC Bank account. To prevent unauthorized access, your account will be suspended within 24 hours unless you verify your identity immediately.

Click the link below to complete verification and restore full access.

Failure to verify will result in permanent account suspension.

HDFC Bank Security Team

redFlags: IRCTC never sends refunds via SMS with a link
Real IRCTC refunds go directly to your original payment method
URL is irctc-refund.in — not irctc.co.in
Creates urgency: expires in 2 hours
Asks you to claim money — banks don't work this way
explanation: IRCTC refunds are automatically credited to your original payment method — they never ask you to click a link to claim a refund. The domain irctc-refund.in has no connection to Indian Railways.
educationTip: No legitimate company will ever ask you to click a link to receive a refund. Refunds are always pushed to you automatically.

data.body: IRCTC: Your ticket refund of Rs.847 is ready. Claim your refund before it expires in 2 hours. Visit http://irctc-refund.in/claim?ref=TXN8847291 - Indian Railways

redFlags: Domain is google-accounts-signin.com — not accounts.google.com
Legitimate Google sign-in is always on accounts.google.com
Subdomain trick: accounts appears in the URL but is not the actual domain
HTTP not HTTPS
explanation: This URL uses accounts as a subdomain to look like Google, but the actual domain is google-accounts-signin.com — a fake site. Google's real sign-in URL is always accounts.google.com.
educationTip: Read URLs from right to left, stopping at the first slash. Everything before that slash is the domain. accounts.google-accounts-signin.com is owned by google-accounts-signin.com, not Google.

data.context: You received this link in an email saying your Google account was accessed from a new device

redFlags: Sender email is orders@amazon-delivery-india.com — not amazon.in
Order number format doesn't match real Amazon
Link domain is amazon-order-verify.com — not amazon.in
Asks to confirm shipping address by clicking — Amazon never does this post-order
Amount is suspiciously round: 4,999
explanation: Amazon's legitimate emails come from amazon.in or amazon.com. The sender domain here is amazon-delivery-india.com — a completely separate site with no link to Amazon.
educationTip: When in doubt about an order, go directly to amazon.in and check My Orders — never click links in emails.

data.subject: Order Confirmation — Your order IN-2024-88471 has been placed
data.body:
Hello,

Thank you for shopping with Amazon India! Your order IN-2024-88471 for Rs.4,999 has been received and is being processed.

Expected delivery: 2-4 business days.

To ensure successful delivery, please confirm your shipping address by clicking the link below. Orders with unconfirmed addresses may be delayed or cancelled.

Amazon Customer Service

redFlags: Zepto never asks you to share your OTP
Legitimate OTP messages always say DO NOT share this OTP with anyone
This SMS asks you to forward the OTP — a social engineering red flag
Fake delivery agent scenario to create a believable reason to share OTP
explanation: This is a smishing attack that tricks you into sharing your OTP under the guise of delivery verification. Real delivery apps like Zepto never require you to share an OTP with a delivery agent.
educationTip: An OTP is a secret between you and the service. No employee, delivery agent, or support person from any legitimate company will ever ask for your OTP.

data.body: Hi, this is Ravi from Zepto delivery. Your order is outside but the app is showing an error. Please share the OTP you just received on this number so I can mark your delivery complete. Sorry for the trouble!

redFlags: Domain is payva1t.com — uses digit 1 instead of letter l (typosquatting)
HTTPS is present — but HTTPS only means the connection is encrypted, NOT that the site is legitimate
Path /securelogin looks convincing but means nothing
Subtle character substitution is a classic hard-to-spot phishing technique
explanation: This URL uses typosquatting — replacing the letter l with the digit 1 in payvault. The site has HTTPS, which tricks many people into thinking it's safe. HTTPS only means your connection to the fake site is encrypted.
educationTip: HTTPS does not mean a website is safe. It only means your connection is encrypted. Scammers can and do get HTTPS certificates for fake sites.

data.context: You received this URL in a WhatsApp message from an unknown number saying your PayVault account shows suspicious activity

explanation: This is a legitimate GitHub security email. The sender domain is github.com, the link goes to github.com, it addresses you by username, uses no urgency tactics, and contains no threats.
educationTip: Not every security email is a phish. Legitimate ones address you by name/username, come from the real domain, and don't threaten you — they just inform you.

data.subject: New sign-in to your GitHub account
data.body:
Hi vijaydev,

A new sign-in to your GitHub account was detected.

Chrome on Windows — Mumbai, India — Today at 2:34 PM IST

If this was you, no action is needed.

If you don't recognize this sign-in, please secure your account immediately by visiting your account security settings.

The GitHub Team

redFlags: UIDAI's official domain is uidai.gov.in — sender is kyc-update@uidai.gov.in.secure-kyc.com
gov.in appears as a subdomain — real domain is secure-kyc.com
UIDAI never sends KYC requests via email
Deadline pressure: 72 hours or services will be suspended
Very convincing formatting and official-sounding language
explanation: This is a highly convincing phish. The sender email contains uidai.gov.in which looks official, but it appears as part of a subdomain — the actual domain is secure-kyc.com. UIDAI never contacts you via email for KYC updates.
educationTip: When an email address contains a legitimate government domain, look at what comes AFTER the last dot before the @ symbol — that's the real domain. kyc-update@uidai.gov.in.secure-kyc.com is owned by secure-kyc.com, not UIDAI.

data.subject: Action Required: Complete your Aadhaar KYC update before 30th November
data.body:
Dear Aadhaar Holder,

As part of the annual Aadhaar data verification drive mandated by the Government of India, all Aadhaar cardholders are required to complete a digital KYC re-verification by 30th November 2024.

Failure to complete this process within 72 hours will result in temporary suspension of your Aadhaar-linked services including:
- UPI and banking services
- Mobile SIM (TRAI mandate)
- DigiLocker access

The process takes less than 2 minutes. You will need your Aadhaar number and registered mobile phone.

Reference: UIDAI-KYC-2024-MH-771234
Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology, Government of India

redFlags: Domain is s1ack.com — digit 1 replaces letter l, very hard to spot in some fonts
HTTPS present and path looks completely legitimate
No other obvious signs — this is a pure typosquat attack
In certain fonts (Arial, Calibri) 1 and l are visually identical
explanation: This is an extremely subtle typosquatting attack. s1ack.com uses the digit 1 instead of l, making it look identical to slack.com in many fonts. There are no other red flags — this is why it's rated hard.
educationTip: For services you use daily, bookmark the official URL and always access them through bookmarks — never from links in messages or emails.

data.context: A colleague sent this Slack link in a WhatsApp message saying 'check this thread, tagged you'

redFlags: Sender domain is infosys-hr.com — real Infosys HR emails come from infosys.com only
Legitimate offer letters are sent via official portals, not raw email attachments
Asks for PAN and Aadhaar via email — this is never done legitimately
No mention of recruiter employee ID or verifiable contact at official Infosys numbers
Urgency: accept within 48 hours
explanation: Job offer phishing targets freshers and job seekers. Infosys HR will only contact candidates from infosys.com domains and through their official hiring portals. Any request to submit Aadhaar or PAN via email is a data theft attempt.
educationTip: Verify any job offer by calling the company's official number listed on their website — not any number provided in the email. Real HR teams expect you to verify.

data.subject: Congratulations! Offer Letter — Software Engineer, Infosys Limited
data.body:
Dear Candidate,

After careful evaluation of your profile and interviews, we are pleased to extend an offer of employment at Infosys Limited.

Role: Software Engineer / Systems Engineer
Location: Bangalore / Hyderabad
CTC: Rs.3,60,000 per annum
Joining Date: January 15, 2025

Please find your official offer letter attached (PDF).

To confirm your acceptance, please reply to this email with the following documents within 48 hours:
1. Aadhaar Card (front and back)
2. PAN Card
3. 10th, 12th, and degree marksheets
4. Passport size photograph

Offer lapses if not accepted within 48 hours.

Infosys Recruitment Team
